statement: Thank you, Lise
Excellencies and friends, it is a true pleasure to be back in London
I was here already in January at the invitation of UN and UK and what I did is I spoke about a world in crisis
Conflicts, famine, climate shocks, whining of global cooperation, the UN under strain
And I wish I could say things have improved
And we know they haven't
That is why I want to speak plainly this evening
Nobody wants another careful speech
What we need is we need action
And I want to focus on four things this evening
First, I want to talk about prevention
The UN was created to prevent war, a solemn promise made when bombs were still raining down in this city
But too often, the UN arrives after the worst has happened
What we need is bold ideas
As Secretary General, I will establish a prevention and early action hub
And its purpose is very simple
To ensure that warnings reach decision makers early and that action can be taken before the situation gets worse
The Secretary-General must always be ready to lead these efforts
She must focus on addressing the consequences of a crisis, whether that's diplomacy to secure life-saving support or working together to manage energy shocks or food shortages
And the Secretary-General must recognise when the UN needs to be centre stage, but also when it's a better place to play a supporting role
Second, we must put people at the heart of development, not the UN
With support from countries like the UK, the UN has saved and transformed millions of lives
We must not forget that
The Sustainable Development Goals remain the right ambition
But ambition is not action
Countries do not need another summit or another slogan
They need the money
the technology and the institutions to deliver for their people
Developments cannot be delivered from New York
I strongly believe that
We need a leaner UN that can focus on where it adds most value, a local first approach that empowers people and governments
Third, we need to work together to harness and manage the power of AI
This is not a tech issue
It is a security issue
It is a jobs issue
It is a rights issue
No single state or company should call the shots
The UN cannot and should not regulate everything, but it must bring people together, including civil society and young people, those that are normally shut out of these conversations
And finally, we have to change the UN for the better, not the cheaper
Yes, we have to cut costs
That is essential
But that cannot be our only goal
Reform must mean doing better
That means focusing on demand, not supply
That means being prepared to let go of some functions entirely and focus on the organization's greatest strengths
Ultimately, you, in this room and outside, will not judge the UN by the number of reform initiatives, nor by whether it has improved your lives and your future
That is the UN I want to build
With governments, with a strong leadership team, and with you, of course
I do not pretend to have all the answers, but this is not a task for one individual
The United Nations is a promise we must all keep
So here's what I offer
A life devoted to serving my country and the international community
An insider-outsider perspective as someone who understands the system but is not captured by it
A track record of working across all the pillars of the UN and with people from all walks of life
a firm commitment without fear or favor to the values of the Charter, to international law, and to serving all Member States and the world 8 billion people
This summer, I suspect many of you will be hoping that England's men's team will lift the World Cup after 60 long years of waiting
Well, it has been 80 years of waiting for a woman to lead the UN
So this is our opportunity to show the UN can walk the talk on equality and results
This is the United Nations I'm prepared to build
Not a perfect institution, but a serious, compassionate one
Not a louder UN, but a more relevant one that delivers where it matters most
I thank you
Well, I think I have the experience to navigate in turbulent waters
I think the UN has never been Disneyland
I think that what is important is to see a secretary general that is impartial, that is able to build bridges, to work with everybody
I have shown that when I was president of the General Assembly
I think we need a secretary general that is
360 secretary-general that knows every pillar of the UN, that is not a person that is not a single-issue person, but more like a Renaissance leader
You also need, what I just said, Liz, you need an insider-outsider, knowing how the UN works, its potential, its challenges, but have, you know, been able to not be captured by the UN
I have never worked for the UN, but served the UN in many ways through the UN for about 25 years
Yes
I'm a poet
I strongly believe in the power of words
I strongly believe in the power of narratives
Communication, but also being faithful to what you say
Okay
And I think it's absolutely important
As a poet, I learn, first of all, to observe, to deep listen, and to act, and act firmly
Okay
And I also, of course, learn about beauty and about creativity and innovation
I think that they're vital
When I think about integrity, I don't think that integrity should be proclaimed
It should be earned
through consistent behavior, and I can show that throughout my 25 years of career and public life
The second very important issue is I would sign the five principles that I read carefully right away, just now
We'll get that from you
When I was exploring the possibility of putting my name forward as Secretary General,
I was the executive director of a very small NGO, not a UN agency
Before I started the consultations, I stepped down from my job just in case there was perception of conflict of interest
The issue of civil society,
It's a little bit of who I am
In civil societies, participation and engagement is not only about ticking the box
It is about understanding that the huge implementation gap of the United Nations
is due to the lack of ownership, the lack of co-responsibility and lack of space of civil society to do the advice, the advocacy, the translation of UN decision-making into the daily life of people
So the role of civil society is absolutely vital
Disclosing the financial, of course, not at the beginning and at the end, but during the process as well, ready to do that, very happy to do that, of course
The veto power
I'm assuming you would want to stop them
What I'm saying is that the veto power is written in the Charter
It's a principle that is in the Charter
And I think we have to be very frank and honest
What is the space of movement of the Secretary General

The Secretary General should provide good offices, create the platforms for member states, especially the P5 and the members of the Security Council, to come together to discuss issues, to respond quickly
That's where my early action hub makes sense, because the role of the UN is to prevent wars and protect people
You don't think there should be a veto when it comes to the worst abuses, the worst violations
I can have my personal views on this, but I think what we're talking here is what is the role of the Secretary General
And the role of the Secretary General is a quiet diplomacy, back channels, to have a permanent and constant interaction and communication with the members of the Security Council, including the P5
Here, my personal opinion doesn't count
I will be the chief administrative officer of the UN, the lead diplomat, the lead advocate, but I cannot reinterpret the Charter, unfortunately
I can use my good offices in talking to the members of the Security Council, especially to the permanent five, of course
It doesn't need to be public
It doesn't need to make headlines
That's part of the job of the Secretary General
No
Yes, all good
Go ahead
Well, now it's in fashion to say, you know, young people are important, youth are important
In the process of negotiating the pact of the future, we came out with a declaration for future generations
And the truth is that young people are not the future, they are the present
They have to be part of the conversation, part of the decision making
If you look at the team that is working with me right now, most of them are under 30
And I listen to them because they are the most creative
They are the smartest, the well-informed
And what would you do to include them
Well, first of all, we have the Declaration on Future Generations, a new and I applaud that a new office for young people
But basically, I would recruit as part of my senior team, young people, I'm working with them right now, and I will ensure
that decision-making in the House brings in the advice, the voice and the space for young people to be part of a new multilateralism of the 21st century
Not to tick the box, but to make them part of the decision-making in the House, respecting, of course, the intergovernmental nature of the United Nations
But they are the agents of today, not of tomorrow
This was in 2013
Thank you, Moon
First of all, I think all good initiatives from the past and the current administration are not a leader of slash and burn
All the good examples, all the good initiatives in the three pillars of the UN, of course, I will embrace them
If they became out of date, I will look at that, of course
But coming back to the human rights issue, this is part of the three pillars of the UN
Human rights is part of the job description of the UN
Everything we do, everything the organisation does, has to really look at human dignity
Human dignity has to be at the centre of everything we do in development, everything we do in peace and security
and believe me
Well, right now I would say every action that the UN does has to embed human dignity and human rights
I don't know if there is, you know, the right environment to have a dedicated initiative
What I can say, however, is the overall budget of the UN depends on how you come from one to three percent of the entire budget of the UN is dedicated to human rights
And this tells you something
We need to do more and better
in a context of austerity that we're living today
Every good idea, every good idea is of course of you, especially if it has to do with strengthening the human rights pillar of the organization
The issue of building networks and partnerships is absolutely vital in the world of today
When we speak about climate change, we understood a long time ago that climate change is not anymore an environmental issue
We understood that climate shocks can trigger conflict, can alter entire macroeconomics of a country
are connected to health, are connected to everything we do
So any response to the climate emergency has to factor in this complexity
But how can you factor in the complexity is to bring all the actors together
You said something very important, Liz
If you ask a person coming from Mars or Jupiter that we have met 31 times soon now in Turkey,
on conference of the parties to discuss and negotiate climate agreements and after 31 years emissions continue to grow
There is a huge implementation gap that we have to deal with and one of the answers to that is
the complexity address climate change from a peace and security perspective, from a conflict perspective, from a health perspective, from a development perspective
It is the defining challenge of our times
Complexity requires complex responses
well organized responses
I have to say within the UN, I would say there is a need for greater coherence, less duplication and better working together within the system
I have seen it from the outside and I think it needs a major restructuring
the UN delivering as one on climate, supporting member states to respond to the climate challenges of today
And just to say one more sentence, I am here representing a small island developing state
I know, I know closely what a climate shock can do
It can devastate and erase the entire infrastructure of a country
So it is the defining challenge of our time
I think the Sustainable Development Goals are the right ambition
I said that
a reference and aspirational agenda
And I think it's very important
It's one of the big successes of multilateralism
And what I would do before we get to 2030 is put in motion the acceleration plans for implementation, of course, but I think that the UN cannot be
an implementer of development projects on the ground
I said development has to be owned by countries because developing countries, and I come from one, we need three things
And that's what I would work on
The money, mobilized resources, the technologies, and the capacity, strong institutions
But the development cannot be delivered from New York
It has to be delivered under the rule of local first, the subsidiarity principle
Well, I will go to the first botanical garden next to me, if it's all buildings, because I think that nature gives you the energy
But I will also listen to elders and the elders that I grew up with
especially the ones I spent five years with in the Ecuadorian Amazon
That was my school, my university, where I learned solidarity and resilience the most
I will call one of them
They do have cell phones these days
At the time where I was living at the Amazon, they didn't
So I will listen to the voices of wisdom and connect to nature
To some tree baby
Thank you
Thank you
